skill.name: authorizing-api-requests
skill.description: Guide Mailtrap API authentication, token scope selection, safe token storage, and account_id resolution before generating or running Mailtrap API calls.
skill.summary: Authorize Mailtrap API requests with scoped tokens, safe storage, and correct account IDs.
skill.author: mailtrap

security_audit.summary: The static analyzer reported many command-execution hits, but most are Markdown inline-code false positives rather than executable Ruby or hidden scripts. The skill is legitimate Mailtrap authorization guidance, but it includes runnable curl examples, bearer-token handling, and command substitution that can affect real Mailtrap accounts if copied without review.

medium_findings.title: Runnable Mailtrap API Examples Use Real Tokens
medium_findings.description: The skill provides curl examples that send bearer tokens to Mailtrap API hosts and can list accounts, access account-scoped resources, or send email when run with live credentials. This is expected for the skill purpose, but users must review endpoints, scopes, and environments before execution.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The curl examples and bearer token headers are directly present, and the skill explicitly covers live sending and account-scoped APIs. The endpoints are official Mailtrap hosts, so the concern is operational misuse rather than malicious exfiltration.

medium_findings.title: Account ID Command Substitution Can Select Wrong Account
medium_findings.description: The account_id helper exports the first account returned by the Accounts API. The skill warns users to filter when tokens can access several accounts, but copying the one-liner unchanged could target the wrong Mailtrap account.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command substitution uses the first array element, and the common mistakes table confirms multi-account ambiguity. This is a clear business-logic risk with documented mitigation in the same file.

low_findings.title: Markdown Inline Code Misclassified as Ruby Backticks
low_findings.description: Static findings for Ruby or shell backtick execution are false positives caused by Markdown inline-code formatting around terms such as account_id, token names, headers, and examples. No executable Ruby file or backtick-based execution was found in the scanned file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited file is Markdown, and the detected backticks delimit documentation text or code snippets. There is no evidence of Ruby execution or hidden script files.

low_findings.title: Secret Storage Mentions Are Defensive Guidance
low_findings.description: The .env, dotenv, secret manager, and 1Password references explain where users should store Mailtrap tokens safely. They do not read secret files or request secret disclosure inside the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines are policy guidance that tells users not to paste or commit tokens. No evidence found of file access, credential harvesting, or unauthorized secret extraction.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs Point to Expected Mailtrap Documentation and APIs
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URL findings reference Mailtrap settings, Mailtrap documentation, and Mailtrap API hosts that match the skill purpose. No third-party collection endpoint or unrelated destination was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: All cited URLs are Mailtrap domains or Mailtrap documentation references aligned with the skill. The network access is expected, but still relevant because live API calls can change account state.

low_findings.title: Unsupported Weak Cryptography and Reconnaissance Flags
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography and system reconnaissance static flags are not supported by the inspected context. The cited lines describe API authorization and a live sending example, not cryptographic algorithms or host reconnaissance.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line evidence contains prose and a Mailtrap send command comment. No evidence found of weak crypto primitives, hashing, scanning, or system enumeration.

dangerous_patterns.title: Bearer Token in Copyable curl Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: Copyable curl examples include Authorization headers populated from environment variables. This avoids literal token exposure, but users can still perform authenticated network actions with real credentials.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is directly visible and semantically aligned with the skill. It is not malicious, but it is a meaningful operational security pattern.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell Command Substitution Around API Response
dangerous_patterns.description: The account_id example uses shell command substitution with curl output piped into jq. This can make a copied command silently depend on the first returned account.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command substitution is explicit, and the surrounding text says users must pick the right account. The risk is misuse of a legitimate helper command.

content.value_statement: Mailtrap API calls fail or create risk when tokens, scopes, and account IDs are chosen incorrectly. This skill guides safe auth headers, secret storage, scoped tokens, and account ID resolution.
actual_capabilities: Explains which Mailtrap auth header to use for new API calls.
Maps Mailtrap token scopes to sending, sandbox, and account-level APIs.
Recommends safe token storage for local, CI, production, and agent workflows.
Shows how to resolve MAILTRAP_ACCOUNT_ID from the Accounts API.
Documents common authorization mistakes and their fixes.
Separates live sending tokens from sandbox testing tokens.
limitations: It does not create or rotate Mailtrap tokens for the user.
It does not validate whether a token has the correct plan or scope.
It does not replace Mailtrap official API documentation for endpoint details.
It includes copyable network commands that require user review before running.

use_cases.title: Prepare a New Mailtrap Integration
use_cases.description: Choose the correct auth header, token scope, and environment variables before writing API calls.

use_cases.title: Secure CI and Deployment Secrets
use_cases.description: Decide how Mailtrap tokens should be stored and injected without exposing them in logs or source control.

use_cases.title: Debug Authorization Failures
use_cases.description: Review token scope, account_id selection, and sandbox versus live token usage when requests return authorization errors.

prompt_templates.title: Choose Auth Header
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for a new request
prompt_templates.prompt: I am calling a Mailtrap API endpoint. Tell me which auth header to use and which environment variable should hold the token.

prompt_templates.title: Pick Token Scope
prompt_templates.scenario: Select least privilege for an integration
prompt_templates.prompt: I need to use Mailtrap for [sending, sandbox, templates, contacts, domains, suppressions]. Recommend the narrowest token scope and explain why.

prompt_templates.title: Review Secret Storage
prompt_templates.scenario: Check a deployment or CI plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my plan for storing Mailtrap tokens in local development, CI, and production. Identify exposure risks and safer alternatives.

prompt_templates.title: Audit API Request Design
prompt_templates.prompt: Audit this Mailtrap API request design for auth header choice, token scope, account_id resolution, sandbox separation, and rotation handling.

output_examples.input: Which token should I use for sandbox testing?
output_examples.output: Use a dedicated sandbox API token. Keep it separate from live sending tokens so a testing leak cannot send real email.

output_examples.input: Why should I not hardcode account_id?
output_examples.output: The account ID can differ by organization and environment. Resolve it from the Accounts API and verify the intended account before writes.

output_examples.input: How should CI receive the token?
output_examples.output: Store the token in the CI provider secret store. Inject it as an environment variable and avoid command-line flags or logs.

best_practices: Use the narrowest Mailtrap token scope that supports the required endpoint.
Keep live sending, sandbox testing, and account-level tokens separate.
Resolve and verify account_id before account-scoped write operations.
anti_patterns: Pasting Mailtrap tokens into prompts, tickets, notebooks, or generated code.
Passing tokens as command-line flags where shell history or process lists can expose them.
Using the first returned account_id without checking account name or access level.

faq.question: Does this skill send email by itself?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and examples. Running the examples with real credentials can send email or access account data.

faq.question: Which auth header should new Mailtrap code use?
faq.answer: Use the Authorization header with a Bearer token unless an older client requires the Api-Token header.

faq.question: Can one Mailtrap token cover every API?
faq.answer: Some accounts can create broad tokens, but narrower tokens reduce the impact of leaks and mistakes.

faq.question: Where should Mailtrap tokens be stored?
faq.answer: Use environment variables backed by a secret store, CI secret manager, or production secret manager. Do not commit tokens.

faq.question: Why does account_id matter?
faq.answer: Many Mailtrap endpoints are account-scoped. The wrong account_id can read or change the wrong account.

faq.question: Is this skill safe for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes, with warnings. It handles sensitive API workflows and includes network command examples that users must review before running.
